data.title: 'The Rise and Fall of Rational Control' Review: To Think and Make It So
data.description:
By Samuel Goldman

Harvey C. Mansfield, who retired in 2023 after 61 years on the Harvard faculty, is the best lecturer I've ever heard. When I was in graduate school two decades ago, attendance in his courses routinely exceeded the official roster. Doctoral candidates, alumni, foreign visitors and even faculty colleagues all appeared in the hall. On a campus where expectations outpace reality, the 75 minutes Mr. Mansfield devoted to Machiavelli or Hobbes felt special.

That's not to say the significance of his lecture was always clear right away. Mr. Mansfield in his classroom teaching made few of the concessions to cultural decline made by most instructors, including this reviewer. There were no potted histories, no schematic definition of concepts, no leading questions to wake up sleepy students. PowerPoint, if he'd heard of it, was unthinkable.

Mr. Mansfield spoke about old books using those books' own words. Because his commentary revolved around the subtleties of their texts, the discussion only began to make sense if you had read them with greater attention than seemed necessary in any other course. It was even more rewarding if you attended his classes on the same topic more than once. The lectures were scholarly achievements in their own right.

"The Rise and Fall of Rational Control," based on Mr. Mansfield's signature course, History of Modern Political Philosophy, known as Government 1061, revises his lectures into chapter-length essays. The book isn't a general introduction to the eight thinkers it addresses. Instead, it presents Mr. Mansfield's interpretation of the meaning, development and crisis of the modern age. Brilliant as his oral presentations were, reading his arguments on the page allows one more easily to grasp their internal logic and unity.

The essence of modernity, Mr. Mansfield argues, lies in the idea of "rational control." Reason, or the human capacity for understanding, is nothing new. Whether or not it is innate, the faculty was recognized by the Greeks when they invented philosophy around the sixth century B.C.

What makes philosophy modern is the insistence that understanding alone is not good enough. By providing knowledge of cause and effect, reason can also help secure the outcomes we want and prevent those we don't. Karl Marx asserted that "philosophers have hitherto only interpreted the world . . . the point, however, is to change it." Mr. Mansfield claims that this aspiration extends all the way back to Niccolò Machiavelli's proposal to replace useless knowledge with the "effectual truth."

Mr. Mansfield is unusual in insisting that the political argument for rational control precedes the technological one — that is, that the urge to generate favorable political outcomes predates the scientific revolution of the 16th and 17th centuries. Not without reason did Machiavelli (1469-1527) compare himself to Columbus and other heroic explorers. The development of modernity, then, consists in increasingly detailed maps of the undiscovered continent that Machiavelli perceived only in part. According to Mr. Mansfield, this process culminates in the blend of limited government, economic commerce and religious toleration typically described as liberal.

Reason promises truth, but liberalism, as Mr. Mansfield describes it, depends on several diversions or, to put it less charitably, frauds. The first, articulated by Thomas Hobbes in "Leviathan" (1651), is a new concept of representation.

Among the basic difficulties of politics is the fact that, while government is evidently necessary, people do not like be ruled. Representation limits this tension by claiming that acts undertaken with the consent of the governed are really their own decisions. Hobbes (1588-1679) concentrated this indirect form of government in an absolute, preferably monarchical sovereign. Later thinkers, including the framers of our Constitution, located power in elected legislators. Representation in the latter sense was more palatable and remains central to the theory, if not always the practice, of modern politics.

The second diversion has to do with the limits of human desire. Aristotle insisted that material possession should properly be limited to the amount required to participate in a self-sufficient community. It is a "very natural and ordinary thing," Machiavelli countered, "to desire to acquire." The problem is that, in a situation of low productivity, the best way to acquire what one wants is to take it from someone else. For Machiavelli, therefore, politics is inseparable from war.

John Locke (1632-1704) responded that mankind can escape this zero-sum nightmare. By applying rational labor to raw materials furnished by nature, we can increase the supply of desirable goods and thus reduce incentives for violence. Economic growth, as we now call it, does not eliminate competition. But it does promote order by redirecting the ambitious few into what Mr. Mansfield calls "the bloodless killing of commerce" while reducing the material deprivation that encourages discontent among the many poor.

That leaves biblical religion as the final obstacle to rational control. If the world is subject to an omnipotent God whose ways are mysterious, it is foolish and even dangerous for human beings to seize control of their own affairs. For Mr. Mansfield, modern political philosophy is atheist at its heart, even though many of its exponents claimed to be believers. Reinterpreting biblical and theological sources in startling ways, these thinkers rejected God as an independent, unpredictable personality who might interfere with man's efforts to achieve mastery of his own predicament.

The modern project, as reflected in the constitutional regimes that emerged in Britain, the Netherlands and eventually the United States, appeared to be a success. But a crisis arose with Jean-Jacques Rousseau (1712-78), the Genevan philosopher who observed that apparent improvements in political, economic and religious conditions had not made men either good or happy. He argued that modern life alienated human beings from their nature. Any savage in the jungle was better off than modern man.

Even as he appealed to the state of nature, though, Rousseau questioned whether it could ever be recovered. Rather than emphasizing unchangeable characteristics, he presented human beings as malleable over long stretches of time. In defiance of the ancient philosophers, he argued that reason was not man's original or definitive capacity. It was rather the product of a particular kind of society.

The rest of "The Rise and Fall of Rational Control," devoted to German thinkers, draws out the implications of Rousseau's extraordinary suggestion. Immanuel Kant (1724-1804), G.W.F. Hegel (1770-1831) and Karl Marx (1818-83) tried to avoid Rousseau's challenge by locating reason in a process of historical improvement leading to a perfected society. Friedrich Nietzsche (1844-1900) drew the radical conclusion that reason interferes with man's highest possibilities. Nietzsche began a new era of postmodern unease that recognizes the limits of rational control but is unwilling or unable to reject it.

Mr. Mansfield's history bears the fingerprints of Leo Strauss (1899-1973), the German-born Jewish scholar who reinvented the study of political thought in the 20th century. Here as elsewhere in his work, Mr. Mansfield credits Strauss's unusual and sometimes controversial approach as his main inspiration. The sharp contrast between ancients and moderns, the tight syllabus of great books and the insistence that these books often bear cryptic meanings — all are Straussian signatures. So is the skepticism of efforts to mediate between faith and reason, and to reconcile divine obligation with human freedom. For example, Mr. Mansfield dismisses the idea of a Judeo-Christian tradition as a Hegelian confection.

Such claims may be provocative even to readers favorably inclined to the book's other arguments. Yet in every case, the effect of Mr. Mansfield's challenges is to compel doubters to return to the books he places at the center of his teaching.

In that sense, "The Rise and Fall of Rational Control" is an exercise in the liberal education that was already threatened when Mr. Mansfield began teaching History of Modern Political Philosophy in the 1960s. You cannot get answers to the questions Mr. Mansfield raises in a textbook or on ChatGPT, or even by examining important passages. The only way to find them is to read, reflect and compare whole books.

The prospects for that sort of learning may seem dim at the moment. In the future, though, old-fashioned reading may turn out to produce benefits that screens and podcasts can't: Only instructors, courses and institutions that require it offer the kind of deeper wisdom some people will always want. Despite the popularity of the course on which it is based, "The Rise and Fall of Rational Control" is dedicated to "those who could not or did not" take Government 1061. I hope it finds its intended audience, extending Mr. Mansfield's career as a master teacher beyond the confines of his time at Harvard.

  • Mr. Goldman is an associate professor of humanities in the Hamilton School for Classical and Civic Education at the University of Florida and a visiting fellow at the American Enterprise Institute.

data.title: Impossible Foods CEO McGuinness to Step Down
data.description:
By Elias Schisgall

Impossible Foods Chief Executive Officer Peter McGuinness is stepping down after nearly four years at the helm of the company.

The plant-based meat-substitutes company said Friday that McGuinness's responsibilities will be handled by Chief Legal & Operating Officer Jason Gao, Chief Demand Officer Meredith Madden and Chief Supply Officer Robert Haas.

McGuinness will remain on the company's board, Impossible Foods said.

"We're grateful for Peter's impactful leadership as CEO, which helped establish Impossible as the strongest player in the category, and we're happy he will remain on the Board," board member Fedele Bauccio said. "We have the utmost confidence in Jason, Meredith, and Rob to lead the company into its next chapter of growth."

Write to Elias Schisgall at elias.schisgall@wsj.com

data.title: The FTSE 100 Index Closes 0.28% Higher
data.description:
The FTSE 100 Index went up by 29 points or 0.28 percent on Friday to close at 10200 points.

The rise was led by Diageo (2.50%), Lloyds Banking (2.49%) and WPP (2.33%).

On the downside, the weakerst performers were Endeavour (-7.17%), Airtel Africa (-5.85%) and Fresnillo (-5.85%).

data.title: Week Ahead for FX, Bonds: U.S. Jobs Data, Central Bank Decisions in Focus
data.description:
By Dow Jones Newswires staff

Below are the most important global events likely to affect FX and bond markets in the week starting Feb. 2.

U.S. jobs data will come back into focus in the coming week as investors assess the likely timing of the next Federal Reserve interest-rate cut after President Trump announced Kevin Warsh as the next Chair once Jerome Powell's term ends in May.

Central bank decisions are due from the European Central Bank, the Bank of England and the Reserve Bank of Australia, with the RBA expected to raise interest rates as inflation rises strongly.

U.S.

Attention turns to key U.S. jobs data after a volatile week for the dollar that saw it drop to its lowest in four years against a basket of currencies before recovering.

Notably, monthly nonfarm payrolls data for January are due on Friday and will give clues on the health of the labor market and the likely consequences for monetary policy.

The Federal Reserve in its recent decision--where it kept interest rates on hold after three consecutive reductions--acknowledged that the labor market was beginning to stabilize. Any confirmation of that view could help the dollar, while a weaker number could reignite speculation about rate cuts.

"The market remains wary of what is happening in the jobs market and was unmoved by the Fed's positive spin, continuing to price two 25 basis-point rate cuts this year," ING economist James Knightley said in a note.

Ahead of that, further indications on the jobs market will come in the form of JOLTS job openings data for December on Tuesday, ADP private payrolls for January on Wednesday and weekly jobless claims Thursday.

Other key indicators on the health of the U.S. economy are due in the coming week, with the latest ISM survey on manufacturing activity in January due Monday, followed by the ISM's services survey on Wednesday. The University of Michigan's preliminary consumer survey for February is due Friday.

The U.S. Treasury's quarterly refunding announcement is due Wednesday.

Investors will also be digesting Trump's choice of Kevin Warsh as Fed Chair to replace Powell when his term ends. Warsh served on the Fed's board of governors from 2006 to 2011.

Canada

Canadian jobs data for January are due on Friday. These come after the Bank of Canada recently kept its policy rate unchanged and will be watched for a signal of where rates go from here.

"Canadian jobs numbers should post another increase in employment with a slight dip in unemployment, which should leave the market continuing to price stable rates through the rest of the year," ING's Knightley said.

Latin America

Mexico's central bank announces an interest-rate decision on Thursday.

Analysts at Citi expect interest rates will be left on hold at 7.00% at this meeting. However, a rate reduction is a possibility due to subdued growth and recent strength in the Mexican peso, they said.

Eurozone

The European Central Bank's meeting on Thursday is the week's key focus in eurozone markets. Rates are expected to be kept on hold with attention tuned to any guidance on the rate outlook and any comments on the euro's recent strength.

"Renewed geopolitical uncertainty and the strength of the euro point to a risk of a slight dovish tilt communications," analysts at BNP Paribas said in a note.

That said, the bar for any policy action is high, and stronger-than-anticipated underlying price pressures suggest the ECB will favor a steady hand for a prolonged period, the analysts said.

Bank of America analysts still expect the ECB to cut rates in March but their conviction is fading, they said in a note.

Data releases include final manufacturing PMIs on Monday and services PMIs on Wednesday for January from Spain, Italy, France, Germany and the eurozone.

Provisional inflation data for January are due from France on Tuesday, Italy and the eurozone on Wednesday. Germany will publish manufacturing orders data for December on Thursday, while France will release industrial production figures for the same month. German and Spanish industrial production data for December are due Friday.

Retail sales data for December will be released by Germany on Monday, and Italy and the eurozone on Thursday.

Germany will auction February 2035 green Bunds on Tuesday and November 2032 conventional Bunds on Wednesday. Spain and France will hold bond auctions on Thursday. The relatively low volume of scheduled issuance keeps the door open for syndicated issues.

U.K.

A rate decision by the Bank of England on Thursday will be the key event in the U.K. in the coming week. Interest rates are expected to be left on hold at 3.75% while the BOE will also announce its latest forecasts for growth and inflation.

In December, the BOE cut interest rates by 25 basis points, but only by a narrow 5-4 majority, as some members remain concerned that inflation is still well above target even as it has declined notably.

This time, policymakers should be less divided "as last month's move may well reduce most members' appetite for another move now," Investec economist Philip Shaw said in a note. Further ahead, rates are likely to fall, however, as inflation drops back, he said.

U.K. economic data in the coming week include Nationwide's house price survey for January and the final estimate of the January manufacturing PMI survey on Monday, followed by final PMI services data on Wednesday.

The U.K. plans to sell Oct. 2035 gilts on Tuesday.

Scandinavia

Denmark will hold a bond auction on Wednesday.

Poland

Poland's central bank announces a rate decision Wednesday.

Rates are likely to be left on hold at 4.0%, ING analyst Leszek Kasek said in a note.

Japan

All eyes are on Japan's Lower House election on Sunday, Feb. 8, which could give the prime minister a stronger mandate to implement economic policies.

Many economists expect the ruling Liberal Democratic Party to regain its majority thanks to Prime Minister Sanae Takaichi's popularity. But markets aren't as enthused by her policy proposals, fearing they could worsen the country's fiscal health. That has led to a selloff in Japanese government bonds and the yen, though prices have stabilized.

The outcome of the election, and any comments from officials about the yen, will be carefully scrutinized for signs on the direction of future policy.

On Monday, the Bank of Japan is scheduled to release a summary of opinions from its January meeting, at which it held its policy rate steady at a three-decade high of 0.75%. Economy watchers look to the summaries to get more insights into the central bank's thinking.

On Friday, investors will also look to any potential remarks from BOJ member Kazuyuki Masu, who is scheduled to meet with local leaders in Ehime, Japan.

The ministry of finance will auction about 2.6 trillion yen of 10-year JGBs on Tuesday and around 700 billion yen of 30-year sovereign securities on Thursday. The 30-year JGBs to be issued in February will be a reopening of the January 2026 issue.

Australia/New Zealand

Australia's central bank is widely expected to raise interest rates Tuesday, but markets are uncertain how aggressive the coming tightening cycle will be.

With inflation back above the RBA's 2% to 3% target band, policymakers will at least need to deliver a hike at the coming meeting, and lace it with hawkish remarks to ensure markets know it is serious about easing price pressures.

But the expected increase in the official cash rate to 3.85% from 3.60% might also cause the RBA some embarrassment, given that it confidently cut interest rates three times last year while forecasting that inflation would remain low.

Inflation has, instead, risen strongly and critics of the RBA will focus on the apparent policy error.

On Friday, senior officials from the RBA, which includes Governor Michele Bullock, will appear before parliament in a three-hour testimony on monetary policy. It is likely to be politically charged if interest rates rise.

In New Zealand, the focus will be on the employment data for the final quarter of last year on Wednesday. Unemployment remains high, but a broad array of economic indicators are now pointing to a recovery in the economy, so there is some chance that the jobless rate will fall from the current level of 5.3%.

China

It is all about PMIs for China, kicking off with official gauges due Saturday, Jan. 31.

The official data is likely to signal that manufacturing sector activity continued to grow at the start of the year, albeit at a soft pace. The reading will be closely watched to see if the rebound seen in December after eight months of negative prints continued.

"This will be one of our first glimpses at China's early economic momentum in 2026," said economists at ING.

A poll of 11 economists by The Wall Street Journal projects that headline PMI likely held steady at 50.1 in January. That would mark a second straight month above the 50 threshold separating contraction from expansion.

Economists at ING also look for a slight uptick in the non-manufacturing PMI, which captures services and construction, to 50.3 from 50.2.

On Monday and Wednesday, private gauges also take the pulse of China's manufacturing and services sectors, respectively.

December's RatingDog manufacturing tipped into positive territory, and another month of momentum will send a positive signal about the health of the economy as 2026 gets underway.

Asia Manufacturing PMIs

A slew of Asia manufacturing PMIs on Monday will show how the year started as tariffs continue to weigh factory activity around the region.

The January surveys for Taiwan, Japan, Korea and other economies will be scrutinized for signals of either better or worsening market conditions.

India

The Reserve Bank of India is due to make its interest rate decision Friday.

Weak inflation over recent months continues to outweigh concerns about the rupee's depreciation and leaves the door open for the RBI to deliver one final rate cut, said Shilan Shah from Capital Economics.

"However, with the economy holding up and inflation set to rise back towards the 4% target, we think this will mark the end of the easing cycle," he said.

South Korea

South Korea is set to post strong export growth in January when it releases trade data on Sunday, Feb. 1.

The median forecast of nine economists polled by The Wall Street Journal is for exports to have increased 30% on year, compared with a revised 13.3% rise in December.

January exports likely got a boost from solid chip demand as well as 3.5 additional working days due to the Lunar New Year holidays shifting to mid-February this year, analysts said.

Imports are projected to have risen 12% on year, resulting in a $6.30 billion trade surplus in January, compared with a revised $12.17 billion surplus the prior month, the poll showed.

On Tuesday, the country will release new inflation data, which will likely show easing price growth in January.

The median forecast from a WSJ poll of nine economists is for a 2.1% on-year rise in the benchmark consumer price index, following a 2.3% increase in December.

That would still be above the central bank's 2.0% target for a fifth consecutive month.

Seasonal inflationary pressures from higher prices of agricultural products and services may have been offset by lower energy prices, according to analysts.

Compared with the previous month, CPI likely rose 0.5% in January after December's 0.3% gain, the poll showed.

Indonesia

Indonesia publishes fourth-quarter economic growth data on Thursday, a release that will probably show that annual growth continued to hover at the 5% mark.

ANZ economists expect the quarterly data to reflect the impulse of a more expansionary fiscal stance, with GDP growth steady at 5.1% versus 5.04% in the third quarter.

Since the finance minister was replaced in September, a stream of stimulus measures have been rolled out and budget disbursement has accelerated, ANZ said. High-frequency indicators also point to firmer consumer confidence and stronger car sales, while net exports likely softened amid weaker commodity demand, it added.

The GDP print will be preceded by inflation and trade data on Monday.

Headline inflation likely rose in January due to base effects from government-administered price measures introduced early last year to support households, while core inflation probably remained relatively steady, Barclays economists said.

December's trade surplus likely widened slightly, as export growth outpaced a contraction in imports. Kenanga Investment Bank expects Indonesia's exports to soften in 2026 amid global uncertainty and the delayed effects of higher U.S. tariffs.

CPIs

A trio of CPI prints are due from Taiwan, the Philippines and Thailand that will factor into interest rate expectations for the three economies.

On Thursday, economists at ANZ expect Philippines inflation data to show that headline CPI eased to 1.6% on the year in January from December's 1.8% expansion. Persistent deflation in rice prices likely helped to contain food costs, while utilities inflation is expected to ease due to lower electricity tariffs.

Thailand's figures are likely to show that deflation pressures have continued at the start of the year. DBS's economics team thinks CPI likely declined 0.1% on the year in January, citing the continued decline in oil prices and muted demand-side impulses.

Taiwan's first batch of inflation data for 2026, on Thursday, is tipped to extend a trend of benign price growth. Forecasts for January put inflation at 1.2% on the year, a touch down from December's 1.31% increase. Stable labor market conditions, a weak property sector, and low international oil prices have all kept the island's consumer prices stable. With inflation staying below the 2% threshold, DBS economists think the central bank could keep the policy rate on hold throughout 2026.

Hong Kong

On Tuesday, Hong Kong retail sales will likely show that the consumption recovery continued in the final month of the year.

Another positive dataset would firm up expectations that the city's economy is out of the woods, coming after GDP data showed improvements in 2025.

DBS economists expect retail sales growth to rise to 7.0% year-on-year in December from 6.5% in November, supported by increased visitor arrivals.

Smaller declines in consumer prices for clothing, footwear, and durable goods also signal improved consumption sentiment, DBS said.

Singapore

Singapore is set to release official PMI data for January on Monday, which will show if manufacturing activity expanded for a sixth straight month.

Sustained momentum in pharma and semiconductors could be critical for the city-state's manufacturing activity, said OCBC chief economist Selena Ling.

Retail sales for December due Thursday will give insight into consumption trends in 2025.

OCBC expects 2025 retail sales growth to have accelerated to 3.5%, eclipsing the 1.4% rise recorded in 2024.

Any references to days are in local times.

Write to Jessica Fleetham at jessica.fleetham@wsj.com and Fabiana Negrin Ochoa at fabiana.negrinochoa@wsj.com

data.title: As bitcoin falls toward $80k, here's why Fed Chair Warsh may not be enough to revive the crypto
data.description:
By Gordon Gottsegen

Bitcoin can get a boost from easing monetary policy, but investors are cautious right now

Bitcoin hit a low of $81,065 today.

It seems like bitcoin can't catch a break.

After dipping below the $90,000 level last week, bitcoin (BTCUSD) fell another 7% this week, hitting an intraday low of $81,065 overnight. This happened against a backdrop of heightened volatility across multiple asset classes, signaling that investors have been feeling risk-averse in recent weeks - and risk-averse investors don't want to park their money in bitcoin.

That may have investors wondering what it would take to get bitcoin out of this rut. Bitcoin was trading at its lowest level since the November 2025 plunge, and may risk dipping below $80,000 for the first time since April 2025's tariff-related selloff, according to Dow Jones Market Data.

It could come down to two things: looser global monetary policy and more risk-friendly investors.

"In my personal view, bitcoin's continued weakness and its consolidation around the 84,000 USD area do not stem from any deterioration in bitcoin's underlying fundamentals," Linh Tran, a senior market analyst at XS.com, wrote in an email. "Instead, this move primarily reflects short-term shifts in the global monetary environment and overall risk appetite."

It's easy to see how risk appetite affects bitcoin. Crypto is typically a volatile asset, and bitcoin often trades similarly to high-beta growth stocks. This month, investors have been showing a decreased tolerance for risk. They have been rotating out of tech stocks and have been diversifying into other parts of the market. On top of that, investors have been moving into metals, which has caused gold, silver and copper to surge.

Tran said that bitcoin faces direct competition from these metals. She noted that geopolitical tensions and policy uncertainty this month - whether that's the military operation in Venezuela, tariff threats over control of Greenland, President Donald Trump's tone at Davos, escalating tensions in Iran and so on - have been driving investors to more traditional defensive assets. While bitcoin bulls believe in the crypto as a store of value, recent market moves signal that the broader investor demographic isn't buying it.

"The fact that gold and other precious metals continue to benefit from policy and geopolitical uncertainty, while bitcoin does not, suggests that investor risk appetite is currently tilted more toward defense than risk-seeking," Tran told MarketWatch.

Related: Former Goldman commodities guru sees another decade of rising prices for metals and critical minerals

A new Fed chair may not change things for bitcoin

Beyond investor confidence, the other piece of the puzzle is monetary policy. Tran said bitcoin is a "a liquidity-sensitive" asset, which means it benefits from central banks cutting interest rates and injecting more money into the global economy.

"In my view, isolated rate cuts can help bitcoin enter a phase of re-accumulation, but they are not sufficient to move bitcoin into a sustainable recovery phase," she said.

Right now, several central banks are leaning more "hawkish" with monetary policy. The Bank of Japan has been raising rates, there's quantitative tightening in Europe and the Federal Reserve held rates steady this week.

In the U.S., there's speculation that the Fed may shift its tone to favor more rate cuts in the future. Trump has been vocal about asking the Fed to lower rates, and on Friday, he picked Kevin Warsh as his nominee to be the new Fed chair when Jerome Powell's term ends in May.

Read: Trump picked Kevin Warsh to boost markets. What this means for your portfolio.

Warsh has a mixed record when it comes to monetary policy. In the past, he's favored rate hikes, but Trump wouldn't have picked him if he expected Warsh to keep rates high.

When it comes to crypto, Warsh has said he views bitcoin as a sustainable store of value, and he's even invested in crypto companies. But a favorable stance on crypto doesn't necessarily mean he'll shift monetary policy to boost it.

But even if Warsh pushes the Fed to cut rates when he joins in May, it'll take further institutional adoption of bitcoin - in addition to monetary policy easing - to push the crypto's price higher.

"In other words, lower interest rates may support bitcoin's rebalancing efforts, but if macroeconomic uncertainty persists and exchange-traded fund flows fail to remain relatively constructive, incremental buying pressure is unlikely to be strong enough to generate a meaningful rebound," Tran said.

-Gordon Gottsegen

This content was created by MarketWatch, which is operated by Dow Jones & Co. MarketWatch is published independently from Dow Jones Newswires and The Wall Street Journal.

data.title: US SEC fraud case against Gautam Adani can proceed after procedural matter resolved
data.description:
By Jonathan Stempel

The U.S. Securities and Exchange Commission has arranged to serve Gautam Adani with a civil fraud lawsuit, allowing the regulator's case against India's second-richest person to proceed.

In a Friday filing in the Brooklyn, New York federal court, the SEC and U.S.-based lawyers for Adani and his nephew Sagar Adani said the lawyers agreed to accept the SEC's legal papers, eliminating the need for U.S. District Judge Nicholas Garaufis to rule on how the defendants should be served.

If the judge approves the resolution, the Adanis will have 90 days to respond to the SEC's complaint, which could include requests for a dismissal.

Robert Giuffra, a lawyer for Gautam Adani, declined to comment. Sean Hecker, a lawyer for Sagar Adani, also declined to comment.

The SEC charged the Adanis in November 2024 with violating U.S. securities law by orchestrating a scheme to pay or promise to pay hundreds of millions of dollars in bribes to Indian government officials to benefit Adani Green Energy , where both are executives and directors.

Both defendants are in India, and the SEC had reported difficulty in serving them with legal papers.

U.S. prosecutors filed a related criminal case in November 2024 against the Adanis and several other defendants. There have been no public developments in that case for more than a year. The SEC's case had been stalled for most of that time.

Gautam Adani, 63, founded and chairs the conglomerate Adani Group. He is worth about $59 billion according to Forbes magazine.

data.title: Stryker's 4Q Results Signal 2026 Growth — Market Talk
data.description: Stryker is positioned for continued growth following a fourth-quarter performance that topped expectations, J.P. Morgan analysts say in a research note. The medical technology firm beat Wall Street estimates for sales and profit, fueled largely by its dominant market share across its MedSurg franchise and hospitals adopting cyclical upgrades to its Mako 4 surgical robots. While the company expects $200 million in new tariff costs in early 2026, management says double-digit growth remains possible. With a healthy order book, the analysts remain bullish that Stryker's strong fundamentals will outweigh near-term headwinds. Stryker is up 3%. (amira.mckee@wsj.com)

data.title: FTC Release: At FTC's Request, Court Halts Operations of Deceptive Health Care Telemarketers
data.description:
Agency alleges Florida-based Top Healthcare Options caused tens of millions of dollars in harm to consumers seeking comprehensive health insurance coverage

At the Federal Trade Commission's request, a U.S. district court in Florida has temporarily stopped the operations of a web of companies and individuals that the FTC alleges caused tens of millions of dollars in harm through the deceptive marketing of health care plans.

data.title: Canadian National Railway Shares Down as 2026 Outlook Disappoints
data.description:
By Adriano Marchese

Canadian National Railway shares declined as softer-than-expected guidance overshadowed strong fourth-quarter earnings.

The stock was off 3.5% at 131.42 Canadian dollars, about $97.42. Shares are down 13% in the past year.

The Canadian railroad expects volume growth in terms of revenue ton miles to be flattish in 2026, while adjusted earnings-per-share growth is forecast to slightly exceed volume growth.

Canadian National's outlook for this year appears to be a little soft, TD Cowen analyst Cherilyn Radbourne said.

"After cutting guidance for 3 years in a row, we were expecting CN would try to underpromise and overdeliver on guidance," Radbourne said in note. TD Cowen felt that mid-single-digit earnings per share growth was realistic, but anticipated the company might guide to low-single-digit growth just to be conservative. "Actual guidance seems to fall short of even LSD growth."

Chief Executive Tracy Robinson said the company expects macroeconomic uncertainty to continue in 2026, along with heightened geopolitical risk. She said the company will go into the year focused on what it can control.

Canadian National's revenue in the fourth quarter rose 2% to C$4.46 billion, in line with forecasts. Net income rose to C$1.25 billion, or C$2.03 a share, up from C$1.15 billion, or C$1.82 a share, a year earlier.

The company said it benefited better freight volumes and improved operational efficiency in the period.

Write to Adriano Marchese at adriano.marchese@wsj.com

data.title: 'Katherine Mansfield' Review: The Woolf of Wellington
data.description:
By Kathryn Hughes

The first meeting between Virginia Woolf and Katherine Mansfield did not go well. She "stinks like a — well civet cat that had taken to street walking," Woolf spluttered to her diary on Oct. 11, 1917. (Woolf was objecting to Mansfield's wearing scent.) She added that Mansfield, a native New Zealander, was "hard & cheap." Though the diary entry concedes that Mansfield was "so intelligent & inscrutable that she repays friendship," the two women wouldn't become friends. Rather, as Gerri Kimber explains in "Katherine Mansfield: A Hidden Life," they would be known as literary sparring partners, both engaged in the high-stakes project of forging a new modernist literature. In their fiction — mostly novels for Woolf, short stories for Mansfield — they experimented by replacing linear narrative and descriptive detail with suggestion and symbolism. Instead of omniscient narrators, they deployed free indirect speech to reveal characters' innermost thoughts. Years later Woolf admitted that Mansfield was the only writer she had ever felt jealous of.

Mansfield had settled permanently in Britain at 19, in 1908. On the run from her well-to-do, stuffy colonial background, she launched herself into London's bohemia. While still in New Zealand, suffocating in suburban Wellington, she had described herself as a "restless soul" with "a rapacious appetite for everything and principles as light as my purse." Now set free, Mansfield seized every experience going. She wrote a little, acted a little, sang a little. She also had a lot of sex, believing that "the idea of sitting still and waiting for a husband is absolutely revolting."

As it turned out, she didn't have to wait long. A year after her arrival, Mansfield married George Bowden, a singing teacher she had met a month before. The omens were not good. The bride wore black and, pregnant with another man's child, made a point of spending the wedding night elsewhere. Recently discovered divorce papers reveal that Bowden blamed the marriage breakdown on Mansfield's "insatiable desires" for sex, which he was unable to satisfy.

Mansfield's family was convinced that she was a lesbian (one of her most intense early affairs had been with a M ori schoolfriend), and believed this was why her marriage to Bowden had faltered. Her mother arrived from New Zealand to sweep her off to a Bavarian spa whose waters were said to cure homosexuality; it was in Bad Wörishofen that Mansfield first read Chekhov and began to write seriously. She produced several short stories, including "Frau Brechenmacher Attends a Wedding," which explores the dynamics within marriage. These appeared in her acclaimed first collection, "In a German Pension" (1911).

Although she never returned to New Zealand, the country of her birth provided Mansfield with her best material. After the death of her beloved younger brother in World War I, she lived imaginatively in the landscape of their shared childhood. The stories she wrote after 1915 — "Prelude," "At the Bay," "The Garden Party" — are among her very best, full of vividly recalled sights, sounds and even scents.

In "The Garden Party" (1922), Mansfield demonstrated her mastery of what today might be called a "cold open." It begins: "And after all the weather was ideal," plunging the reader headfirst into the collision between preparations for a party and funeral preparations for a young man killed in an accident.

Mansfield met her second husband, the editor John Middleton Murry, when she submitted a short story to his avant-garde magazine, Rhythm. They embarked on a painfully destructive intermittent relationship, which culminated in an unhappy marriage in 1918. Both were spectacularly unfaithful, but Murry's bad behavior was compounded by psychological sadism. On one occasion, after Mansfield had been diagnosed with tuberculosis and fled to the French Riviera in an attempt to heal, Murry let slip that he had discussed setting up home with his mistress, the aristocratic Dorothy Brett.

Virginia Woolf went on to publish modernist masterworks including "Mrs. Dalloway" (1925) and "The Waves" (1931); Mansfield never lived to do so. Her tuberculosis killed her in 1923, at the age of 34. Ignoring Mansfield's dying instructions "to tear up and burn as much as possible, " Murry published his wife's journals and letters, editing them to ensure that she emerged as a figure of romantic tragedy. All evidence of Mansfield's busy sex life, not to mention her malicious wit and suicidal episodes, were excised. Naturally, the unhappiness of her marriage to Murry was nowhere in sight. Contemporaries were convinced that his motivation for publishing Mansfield's ephemera wasn't so much reverence for her talent as greed for hard cash. The critic Sylvia Lynd accused Murry of "boiling Katherine's bones to make soup"; D.H. Lawrence said he "made capital out of her death."

Since the late 1970s, a number of biographies of Mansfield have sought to dismantle the sanitized version of her life promoted by Murry, and a scholarly industry devoted to restoring her unadulterated voice has flourished. Ms. Kimber, a visiting professor of English literature at the University of Northampton, has dedicated her academic career to ferreting out overlooked documents, and it is one such discovery — ironically not by her — that she makes the central plank of this biography.

Drawing on work by the scholar John Wood, Ms. Kimber proposes that Murry was not Mansfield's most significant emotional and professional relationship; that claim properly belongs to a man called A.R. Orage, editor of the New Age magazine, which published some of her earliest stories. In addition to documenting Orage's literary mentoring of the young Mansfield, Ms. Kimber claims that the two had an affair. The evidence for this seems slim. More certain is that Orage stayed in touch with Mansfield throughout her life. He introduced her to the teachings of George Gurdjieff, the esoteric spiritual teacher who took Western intellectual circles by storm in the 1920s. Mansfield was "fascinated" by Gurdjieff's ideas, according to Ms. Kimber; when she died, she was at the Institute for the Harmonious Development of Man in Fontainebleau, outside Paris. She had gone there in a last-ditch attempt to recover her health and instead collapsed with a violent pulmonary hemorrhage.

This discovery of Orage's significance in Katherine Mansfield's life and work matters to scholars, but Ms. Kimber misjudges how much it might engross readers of a biography. Her preoccupation with archival minutiae fills space that could have been better spent. The reader doesn't discover much about how Mansfield's work was shaped by changing personal circumstances, from hectic bohemia to a series of continental spas, and learns too little about Mansfield's achievements as a literary provocateur. Katherine Mansfield could write wonderful lines such as: "The men walked like scissors; the women trod like cats." I finished this book still wanting to know how she did it.

  • Ms. Hughes is the author of "Catland: Louise Wain and the Great Cat Mania."

data.title: CVLG: Freight revenue up, margins down; Star acquisition and market recovery expected to drive 2026 gains
data.description:
Freight revenue grew 7.8% year-over-year, but adjusted operating income fell 39.4% due to margin compression. The Star Logistics acquisition diversifies revenue and is expected to be accretive in 2026. Management is optimistic about improved market conditions and margin recovery later in the year.

data.title: Chevron CEO says can process another 100,000 bpd of Venezuelan crude at its refineries
data.description:
Chevron CEO Mike Wirth said on Friday the U.S. oil major can process an additional 100,000 barrels per day of Venezuelan crude at its refineries.

The company currently processes 50,000 barrels a day of Venezuelan crude at its Pascagoula refinery on the U.S. Gulf Coast, Wirth said.
